author: @foogod4237
text:
Technically, this isn't actually a battery, but a generator.

Batteries directly convert chemical energy to electricity (and vice-versa).  This is going through a separate step of converting chemical energy to heat, and then using that heat to generate electricity (similar in some ways to how a steam engine or nuclear power plant works, just using the stored chemical energy in the dry clay for a power source instead).

One of the reasons the distinction is important is because this process also makes it much much less efficient than a typical battery would be...

(But it is still a kinda neat effect)

author: @josephdupont
text:
The number of moles of beryllium (B) that can be released from a pound of dry clay under ideal conditions depends on the specific composition of the clay and the method used to extract the beryllium. Clay is a complex mixture of minerals and organic materials, and the presence of beryllium in clay can vary widely depending on its geological origin.

To determine the amount of beryllium that can be released from a pound of dry clay, a chemical analysis of the clay would typically be required. This analysis would involve techniques such as X-ray fluorescence (XRF) spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma (ICP) spectroscopy, or other analytical methods to determine the beryllium content of the clay.

Once the beryllium content of the clay is known, it would be possible to calculate the number of moles of beryllium in a pound of dry clay using the molar mass of beryllium. Beryllium has a molar mass of approximately 9 grams per mole.

Without specific information about the beryllium content of the clay, it is not possible to provide an exact answer to this question. However, it's important to note that beryllium is a toxic element, and proper safety precautions should be taken when working with materials that may contain beryllium.

author: @danmyself5341
text:
This is called a TEG, A THERMAL ELECTRIC GENERATOR. It generates electricity due to the Seebeck effect. The efficiency on average is 4.5%. they require a large difference in temperature to be effective.
They are not commercially viable due to the fact that they use a rare chemical called tellurium.
They are prone to failure because the substrate used to sandwich the chemicals are ceramic, and can crack.
The cells are built in series, so if one cell fails, it a all fails.

author: @JeremyEllwood
text:
When I was in college (first year), I was bored in my chemistry class (was going for vet-med). My instructor knew that I shouldn't be in that class and that the test to test out was stupid and asinine. 
So we had a very huge mutual respect.

One day, she was giving her lecture and I went over to the lab table, ignoring the lecture and started running water. I saw her peering at me as she lectured. I put on a glove and grabbed a tiny piece of potassium and a scalpel. 

Once the water was at an acceptable level, I turned it off and shaved off a small piece of potassium and tossed it in the water.

Results happened. And without skipping a beat in her lecture, the prof said, "And that is why we don't throw potassium into water..." and then just continued with her lecture.

I got a fun smirk from her and needless to say, aced the class.

I say that to say this: This dude reminds me a lot of her. Excitement over what could otherwise be considered mundane science. But it's not. It's fascinating... even if we still understand it fully, it's god damned fascinating.

author: @AJTarnas
text:
the clay was baked. it takes significantly more high grade energy to charge it than you get by discharging. you get mostly lowgrade heat discharging. it's not too different from acting amazed that quicklime or portland cement gives off heat. the electrical energy that can be recovered from quicklime on a small scale is miniscule and irrational. likewise with your clay. 

but it's a nice party trick. and a tool to have in the kit. there are potentially rare situations where this sort of power source makes sense. in a broad sense of course all batteries and capacitors and heat sources have this general framework in common. but to "charge" clay requires much different roundtrip efficiency than most secondary chemical cells. 

i really wish you wouldnt bury these details in an overly exuberant short video. you lose nothing by being more honest, long term.

author: @zenmanproject
text: The only issue with this is that TEG is made from quite a few rare earth elements such as Tellurium, Bismuth, Selenium and Antimony. There's not enough Te, Se or Bi on earth to make anything of mass scale. Tin and Sulfur make the most efficient TEG but the temperatures needed are quite high. What we need is a TEG that operates at temperatures like Bismuth Telluride but are made from elements like carbon, silicon and sulfur. Those are in the same columns on the Periodic table as Tin and Sulfer, it's possible in the right combination they'll have similar thermoelectric properties.

author: @Smo1k
text: An interesting use of materials like zeolite is as thermal stabilization: The water and clay are kept in a closed container made of something which transports heat well. When it's hot outside the container, that heat goes into evaporating the water, but when it's cold enough for the water to condensate, the container heats up. But for that use, I'd probably be looking at ferrosulphate, since the tipping point for that is at some 60C or so, a temperature you can quite often reach on a sunny rooftop.

author: @fyremoon
text: There is a synthetically produced zeolite clay material called a molecular sieve. These have pores in the material that adsorb water (and other chemicals depending on the diameter of the pores) which produce intense heat when exposed to water. They are used as desiccants to create very dry environments and adsorb more water faster than silica gels, and they are reusable. You can activate them by drying them with a heat source like a rocket stove for a few hours then store them in a dry enclosure until they are needed.

author: @markwarburton8563
text: That's a great video, Rob, thanks! It's got me thinking about using the wet zeolite and cooling it off in the wind and/or sunlight to generate a cold side for the Peltier (I saw you do that in an earlier video). It could be mounted on the roof in a weather-vane type arrangement to get the best wind exposure. This could then be used alternately from hot to cold and cold to hot. The thing is that the Peltier doesn't need to be swapped and the zeolite doesn't either. The only difference is exposing one side to wind and the other to water, then switch sides and do it all over again. There's no free lunch, so we'd need a constant (small) supply of water and the Peltier isn't very efficient, but as a proof of concept that would be very good. We could always go with a Stirling engine for better efficiency and greater wattage.

author: @AcvaristulLenes
text:
After water evaporates, you're left with clay + minerals (mostly calcium).
Repeat the process a few times and performance goes down.
Your choices are:
1. use distilled water
2. you're lucky enough to live somewhere with very soft water, thus the battery's life will be extended.
No matter the case, we should evaluate the energy needed to produce the thermoelectric generator and the energy we get during the lifespan of the clay.
Also, what happens with the expended clay? Does it get dumped, dumped + trash-managed, recycled, reused? How much energy will those steps take?
Yes, I understand that it's an interesting fact but, to me, it feels like presenting how to cut cake with a chainsaw. It's possible but far from being worth the effort.

replies.author: @ModelLights
replies.text:
'to dry again.'  Most any battery tech has a 'downhill' slide.  You're not just wetting this to produce electricity, when you wet it a chemical change is happening to make the electricity.  Like iron rusting, aluminum oxidizing, etc.  That part tends to not go backwards just because you later dry it out, eventually you will use up whatever is actually producing the energy.
May still be a fine, cheap and easy idea.  But pretty much guaranteed there is something else going on besides only wet and dry.
And realize, spinning a motor is low energy, heating and cooling is not.  This is probably a very low energy production system.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @ModelLights You are correct that over time, there will probably be some undesired chemical reactions. Nevertheless, the heat released by anhydrous Zeolite is due to the "Heat of Adsorption" which means that in the process of water molecules adhering to the surface of the zeolite, it releases heat. This can be "recharged" simply by removing the water, and then heating it sort of like how you might heat silica. So this isn't a "battery" in the traditional sense of redox reaction.

author: @graysonsmith7031
text: Have you thought about something like a thermal fluctuation generator? Basically something black and insulated so it collects and stores solar thermal energy, then at night it radiates off that heat through a peltier to produce electricity. Using a geothermal loop or night sky radiator could decrease the temperature of the cold side/time to increase power output. It could even operate strictly off of the change in temperature of the air from night to day. Energy could be generated as the thermal mass heats up (assuming the only route for thermal energy both ways is through the peltier) and as it cools down. That way you could generate energy both night and day with little more than a peltier and a barrel of sand, water, or wax (as a phase change material).

author: @twidgevr
text:
Great stuff! Your last couple of videos have had me dreaming up ideas to make use of this adsorption energy, but in the end it really is just a battery - the desorption process takes the same amount of energy as the adsorption process.  The fresnel trick had me wondering if you can take advantage of the adiabatic cooling and have a dual process going on, with hot zeolite on one side being wetted and cool zeolite on the other being dried, would this be able to improve the output of the peltier device?

In the end it looks like the best way to dehydrate it is with solar energy, and it seems like you may be better off using that energy directly via PV than collecting scraps with TEGs, though.  Do you have any suggestions for ways I'm not thinking of to efficiently dry the zeolite?

It is heat treated to force any water out, but this stuff is a dessicant, it will gladly pull moisture out of the air while you're not looking.  Robert breifly showed drying the zeolite with a fresnel lens, but generally you have to heat it above 250C+ for a length of time to 'recharge' it.

replies.author: @twidgevr
replies.text: The research spiral this sent me down had me reading a paper where someone coated the backside of a solar panel in zeolite, and then coated that with a film of ammonium nitrate, as self-recharging cooling for the panel.  During the day heat is added which forces water out of the zeolite, cooling it, and then the water dissolves the ammonium nitrate resulting in even further cooling.  At night, the water is re-adsorped into the zeolite, forcing the ammonium nitrate out of solution to solidify again.  Rinse, repeat.
